narrative:
Gladepoint Strategies is a five-person strategy consultancy founded in
2020, working with mid-market software and industrial clients. The firm
runs lean, distributed, and calendar-driven: engagement letters go out as
signed PDFs, kickoff memos and status reports live in Word, steering
updates are short PowerPoint decks presented over video calls, and a lot
of the actual coordination happens in brisk email threads between the
engagement lead, the client sponsor, and the team. The Managing Director
signs the letters; principals run the work. Tone is contemporary,
plainspoken business English: short paragraphs, concrete verbs, first
names internally, full names and titles when a client is on the page.
Spreadsheets are tidy and annual.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0003",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Engagement Letter: Pricing Architecture for Brewer, Manning and Davis

authors.name: David Jones
authors.title: Managing Director
authors.persona: David is the firm's public face and final signature: he opens doors with mid-market executives, sets the terms of every engagement, and reads everything that carries the Gladepoint name before it leaves the building. His edits are surgical and his emails rarely run past three short paragraphs. He is warm on a call, formal on the page, and allergic to jargon; when a letter matters, he rewrites the closing himself.

participants.name: Caroline Mayo
participants.title: Principal
participants.persona: Caroline runs engagements end to end and is the intellectual engine behind most of the firm's client work. She thinks in frameworks but writes in plain sentences, and she pushes teams to state the recommendation in the first line rather than bury it. With clients she is direct and unhurried, comfortable delivering hard news; internally she fires off terse, first-name emails that get straight to what she needs and by when.

participants.name: Jeff Pope
participants.title: Engagement Manager
participants.persona: Jeff is the connective tissue of an engagement: he owns the calendar, the status reports, and the email threads that keep the client sponsor and the team in sync. His writing is brisk and organized, heavy on short bulleted updates and clear asks, and he has a knack for translating Caroline's thinking into something a steering committee can absorb in a glance. Colleagues know him as the one who follows up, politely and relentlessly, until the loose end is tied.

docs.engagement_summary: Pricing Architecture for Brewer, Manning and Davis, running about 4 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.

calendar date; place where the date belongs

docs.guidance: Formal letter on firm letterhead confirming the engagement: scope, approach, team, fee and start date, closing. End with a sigblock block signed by the letter's author and the client contact. 3-6 paragraphs.
